title: Sealing the gap under skirting boards
subtitle: Caulking or mastic to close the junction between skirting and floor
excerpt: The gap between skirting boards and floorboards is one of the most common and overlooked draught paths in UK terraced houses. It takes under an hour per room to seal with a tube of flexible sealant, with payback inside the first heating season.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance for floor-edge sealing. Historic England guidance on sealing timber-framed and traditional buildings without trapping moisture.

glossaryTerms.definition: Materials and methods for sealing unintentional air gaps in the building envelope, including door frames, window perimeters, floor edges, and chimney flues.

content.text: The gap between the bottom of a skirting board and the floor surface connects the room air directly to the under-floor void (on suspended floors) or to the cavity behind the plasterboard (on stud walls). On a cold day, air moves through this path by convection: warm room air rises, cool air enters through floor-level gaps. Sealing it with flexible mastic or caulk takes an hour per room.
Which material to use
Flexible acrylic decorator's caulk: paintable, fast-drying, suitable for clean smooth gaps with movement of less than 2 mm. The default for skirting-to-plaster or skirting-to-hardwood situations.
Silicone sealant: more flexible, slightly translucent, not paintable. Better for skirting-to-vinyl, skirting-to-ceramic, or anywhere the gap moves seasonally.
Draught-excluder foam tape or brush strip: use only when the gap is larger than 5 mm and mastic would be insufficient. Press-fit into the gap; trim flush with a utility knife.
Preparation
Step 1, clean the joint.

Brush away dust and debris from the gap with a stiff brush or vacuum nozzle. Any grit left in the joint prevents adhesion. If there is old flexible filler or paint that is cracked and lifting, remove it with a utility knife.
Step 2, mask if painting.

If you want a clean painted line, apply masking tape to the floor surface 2-3 mm from the skirting and to the skirting face 2-3 mm from the floor. Peel off the tape while the caulk is still wet for a clean edge.
Applying the sealant
Step 3, cut the cartridge nozzle at 45 degrees.
Cut the nozzle of the sealant cartridge at a 45-degree angle with a utility knife, making the opening approximately 4 mm wide. Load into the sealant gun.
Step 4, run a continuous bead.
Hold the gun at 45 degrees to the joint and apply a continuous bead along the gap, moving at a steady rate. Work in runs of 1-2 m, then tool (smooth) with a dampened finger before it skins over.
Step 5, check for gaps.

On a cold day after the sealant has set, hold the back of your hand close to the joint with the heating on. Any remaining cool draught will be detectable. Larger gaps (5+ mm) can be filled with a backing rod of closed-cell foam cord before the sealant goes on, to avoid using excessive material.
Suspended floors: the bigger picture
Sealing the skirting joint reduces air infiltration, but on a suspended timber floor the floor surface itself also leaks air through the gaps between floorboards. A full
draught-stripping

approach on a suspended floor also includes filling the gaps between boards (floor-gap filler, paper-pulp filler, or flexible sealant for wider gaps) and sealing all pipe and cable penetrations through the floor. The skirting joint is the highest-return single step; the inter-board gaps are the second priority.

items.cause: Gap moved seasonally (timber floor expanding/contracting) more than the caulk can accommodate
items.fix: Use a flexible silicone or acrylic-silicone hybrid rather than a standard decorator's caulk. The flexibility rating is printed on the tube.

items.symptom: Sealant will not adhere to the floor surface
items.cause: Floor polish, wax, or silicone contamination from previous treatments
items.fix: Clean with white spirit, let dry fully, then apply. Check that the sealant is compatible with the floor type (some silicones are incompatible with cork).
